title: Quiz: The SIDH Attack
description: Test your understanding of the SIDH attack

questions.question: When was the SIDH attack discovered?
questions.explanation: Castryck and Decru published their attack in July 2022, after SIDH had been studied for 11 years. The attack was quickly verified and improved by the community.

questions.question: What enabled the SIDH attack?

options.text: The auxiliary torsion points φ(P), φ(Q)

questions.explanation: The attack exploited the auxiliary torsion points that SIDH published for protocol correctness. This extra information created a rich algebraic structure that could be attacked.

questions.question: What mathematical technique does the attack use?
questions.explanation: The attack uses Kani's theorem (1997) to construct higher-dimensional abelian varieties, then Richelot isogenies to find splittings that reveal the secret.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the SIDH attack?
questions.explanation: The attack runs in polynomial time - approximately O(a³) where 2^a is the isogeny degree. This is devastatingly faster than the expected 2^a security.

questions.question: How long did it take to break SIKE p434 after improvements?
questions.explanation: After improvements by multiple researchers, SIKE p434 could be broken in under 10 minutes on a standard laptop computer.

questions.question: What happened to SIKE after the attack?

options.text: It was withdrawn from NIST consideration

questions.explanation: NIST officially withdrew SIKE from consideration. The attack is fundamental to the protocol design and cannot be patched - SIDH is completely broken.

questions.question: What is a key lesson from the SIDH attack?

options.text: Auxiliary data can enable unexpected attacks
Quantum computers are faster than expected

questions.explanation: A key lesson is that auxiliary data (like φ(P), φ(Q)) published for protocol correctness can create attack vectors. Minimizing published information is important.

questions.question: The SIDH attack requires a quantum computer.
questions.explanation: False! The attack is entirely classical - it runs on regular computers. This makes it even more devastating: SIDH was supposed to resist both classical and quantum attacks.
